title: Session 7: AI in Research Practice
text:
NoteThis session is under development



Content for this session will be added before April 29, 2026. The outline below reflects the planned structure.

title: Session 7: AI in Research Practice
text:
Planned content

AI for academic writing (~15 min)

Using LLMs to draft, edit, and restructure prose.
Limitations: generic phrasing, hallucinated citations, loss of voice.
Demo: paste a rough paragraph, ask for revision, compare.



AI for literature review and discovery (~15 min)

Tools for searching and summarizing papers: Semantic Scholar, Elicit, Connected Papers, Consensus.
Demo: find papers on a topic, generate a summary table.
Caveat: always verify the citations exist.



Journal and university policies (~10 min)

AEA, Nature, Science, PNAS disclosure requirements.
Cornell’s academic integrity policies as they apply to AI use.
Your responsibility as the author: AI-generated content does not transfer authorship responsibility.



Research transparency in the AI era (~10 min)

How to document AI use in your research workflow.
Logging prompts and outputs as part of a replication package.
Reproducibility challenges when AI tools change over time.
Connection to Sessions 4–5: tagged Git releases as replication snapshots that include AI-assisted code.



Discussion and practical exercise (~20 min)

Ethical scenarios: When is AI use appropriate? When does it cross a line?
Cases: using AI to write a referee report, generate code for a replication package, draft an abstract.



Wrap-up (~5 min)

Synthesis of the module.
Key takeaways for working with AI in research over your career.

title: Practical Skills: Git, GitHub & AI
text: This site covers sessions 4–7 of AEM 7010. Sessions 1–3 (introduction and reproducibility with Lars Vilhuber) and session 8 (Cornell resources) are taught separately and are not included here.

title: Practical Skills: Git, GitHub & AI
text: This site covers sessions 4–7 of AEM 7010. Sessions 1–3 (introduction and reproducibility with Lars Vilhuber) and session 8 (Cornell resources) are taught separately and are not included here.

title: Practical Skills: Git, GitHub & AI
text:
What you will learn








Module
Sessions
Topics




Version Control
4–5
Git fundamentals, GitHub, branches, pull requests, tags, collaboration


AI Tools
6–7
Coding assistants, research workflows, disclosure, verification



By the end of these four sessions you will be able to:

Track changes to your R scripts and research code using Git
Back up your work on GitHub and collaborate with co-authors through pull requests
Tag specific versions of your code for paper submissions and replication packages
Use AI coding assistants responsibly in research
Document and disclose AI use in a way that meets journal and replication standards

title: Practical Skills: Git, GitHub & AI
text:
Sessions
Session 4: Git Fundamentals. The core Git workflow on your own computer: init, add, commit, log, diff, .gitignore, and undoing mistakes.
Session 5: GitHub & Collaboration. Connect to GitHub, push and pull, create branches, open pull requests, and tag releases for reproducibility.
Session 6: AI Foundations. The landscape of AI coding assistants, how they work, and a live demo of AI-assisted R coding.
Session 7: AI in Research Practice. Journal and disclosure policies, documenting AI use in replication packages, and ethical scenarios.

title: Practical Skills: Git, GitHub & AI
text:
Before You Begin
Please complete the following before Session 4.

Install Git on your laptop

Mac: brew install git (if you have Homebrew) or download from git-scm.com
Windows: download the installer from git-scm.com

Open a terminal

Mac: open the Terminal app (search for “Terminal” in Spotlight)
Windows: open Git Bash (installed with Git)

Verify the installation by running:

git --version
You should see something like git version 2.39.0.

Install R and an editor or IDE. Sessions 4–5 show three ways to use Git: Terminal, RStudio, and VS Code. You will follow the Terminal in class, but having an editor also set up lets you try the GUI alternatives at home. Sessions 6–7 assume you have both editors available for the AI-coding demos.

R is required for every code example in this site and for the wooldridge package. Download the installer for your platform from cran.r-project.org. Free and open source.
RStudio is the standard IDE for applied economics research in R. If you already use it for coursework, you are set. Otherwise, download from posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop.
VS Code is a general-purpose code editor that has become the most widely used tool in software and data work. Download from code.visualstudio.com.

I encourage you to install all three. R is the engine. RStudio is ideal for R-centric work. VS Code is ideal for everything else: Python, shell scripts, LaTeX, Quarto, Markdown, editing configuration files, and working with AI coding assistants.
Install the wooldridge R package so the tutorial’s data examples work without any downloads. In R or RStudio, run:
install.packages("wooldridge")
This gives you the classic datasets from Jeffrey Wooldridge’s Introductory Econometrics textbook (wage1, wage2, and many others) that we use throughout the exercises.

Before Session 5, also create a free GitHub account at github.com.






NoteWhat is Homebrew?



Homebrew is the standard macOS package manager. Think of it as an app store for command-line tools. If you do not already have it, run the one-line install command from brew.sh. If you prefer to skip Homebrew, use the direct download from git-scm.com instead.

title: Practical Skills: Git, GitHub & AI
text:
Companion Materials

Session 4 slides (PowerPoint)
Session 5 slides (PowerPoint)
Exercise R scripts

title: Session 4: Git Fundamentals
text:
Version control is a systematic record of how your code changes. It matters in three situations every applied economist now faces.


If your project folder has ever looked like this:
analysis_v1.R
analysis_v2.R
analysis_FINAL.R
analysis_FINAL_v2.R
analysis_REALLY_FINAL.R
analysis_FINAL_USE_THIS.R
…then you already know the cost. You made choices you cannot reconstruct. You changed something that worked and cannot remember what.
Git replaces this chaos with a clean history of snapshots. Each snapshot (called a commit) records exactly what changed, when, and why. You can return to any previous version at any time.
This matters especially for managing paper submissions. Every paper moves through several versions of code: the first submission, the R&R revision, the final accepted version, the posted replication package. A reviewer asks, three months after you submitted, “what exactly did you do for the coefficient in Table 2?” Without version control, answering that question means digging through emails and folders. With Git, you mark each submission with a tag (covered in Session 5) and return to it in one command, even years later.



Increasingly you will write research code alongside AI coding assistants: Claude Code, GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Codex, and similar tools. These systems produce dozens of lines in seconds. Most of it is useful; some of it is subtly wrong in ways you will not catch on first read.
Version control is what makes this workflow safe. Every AI-assisted change becomes a commit you can review before accepting, revert cleanly when a test breaks three hours later, or roll back selectively while keeping the parts that worked. It also produces a written record of what came from a model and what you wrote yourself. That record is increasingly expected by journal disclosure policies and by AEA replication review.



Applied economics papers increasingly have two, three, or four co-authors writing code together. Someone edits the cleaning script on Monday; someone else reworks the regression specification on Tuesday. Without version control, the only way to combine those edits is to email files back and forth. That process silently destroys work.
Git (and GitHub, which we cover next session) makes collaboration explicit. Every change is attributed to a specific author, every conflict between edits is surfaced rather than overwritten, and every co-author sees the same history.






NoteThese three motivations connect the whole course



Yourself over time is the reproducibility theme (Sessions 2–3 with Lars). AI tools are Sessions 6–7. Co-authors are Session 5 (GitHub). Version control is the connective infrastructure that makes all three workable.

title: Session 4: Git Fundamentals
text:
What Is Git?
Git is a distributed version control system. It takes a snapshot of your entire project every time you commit. Think of it as a timeline:
v1: Load data  →  v2: Clean variables  →  v3: Run regression
Each point on the timeline is a commit. Git stores the full history locally on your computer.






Important



Git ≠ GitHub. Git is the tool that runs on your computer. GitHub is a cloud hosting service where you can store and share your Git repositories online. We cover GitHub in Session 5.

title: Session 4: Git Fundamentals
text:
Setup
You should already have Git installed from the pre-class setup on the Preliminaries page. Let’s confirm, then configure your identity.

Confirm Git is installed
git --version
You should see something like git version 2.39.0. If you get “command not found”, go back to the Preliminaries page and follow the install instructions before continuing.


Configure your identity
Git attaches your name and email to every commit.
First, check whether you already have this set. Many students have Git configured from earlier coursework or other projects.
git config --global user.name
git config --global user.email
If both commands return your name and email, you are already configured. You can skip the rest of this section.
If either command returns nothing, set them now:
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "you@email.com"
Replace "Your Name" and "you@email.com" with your actual name and email. Git stores these globally, so you only do this once per machine.






TipWant to see everything Git has configured?



git config --global --list
This prints your full global configuration (name, email, default editor, default branch name, and any other settings). Useful for diagnosing “why is Git acting strange” later.








NotePrivacy: your email becomes public on every commit



If you plan to push your work to public GitHub repositories, the email you set above will be visible in the commit log to anyone who views the repo. If you prefer to keep your real email private, GitHub provides a stable noreply address you can use instead. We cover the setup in Session 5.

title: Session 4: Git Fundamentals
text:
The Three Areas of Git
Every Git project has three areas. Understanding them is the key mental model:








Area
What it is
How you interact




Working Directory
The files you see and edit
You edit files normally


Staging Area
A holding zone for the next commit
git add moves files here


Repository
The permanent history of commits
git commit saves a snapshot



The workflow is: edit → stage → commit.






TipA mental model: the shopping cart



Think of Git’s workflow as online shopping.

Working directory is browsing the store and dropping items into your cart. You can add, remove, change your mind freely.
Staging area is the checkout page where you review your cart before ordering. You can still remove things or add more.
Repository is your order history. Every order is timestamped and permanent.

The command mapping is straightforward:

git add moves a file to the checkout page.
git restore --staged removes a file from the checkout page (we cover this later).
git commit presses Place Order. You get a confirmation number (the commit hash), and the order joins your permanent history.
git log is your order history page.

The staging step exists for the same reason stores have a checkout review: before you make anything permanent, you want to see exactly what you are about to commit to.

title: Session 4: Git Fundamentals
text:
Your First Repository






WarningKeep Git repositories out of cloud-sync folders



Before creating your first repository, make sure you are not inside Dropbox, Box, iCloud Drive, OneDrive, or Google Drive. These services can race against Git’s internal file writes and silently corrupt your repository. GitHub itself is the cloud backup for Git; layering another cloud sync on top creates conflicts.
A common convention is to keep all Git projects in a single dedicated folder in your home directory, for example ~/github/. Each project is a subfolder of that.
If you do not have this folder yet, create it now:
mkdir ~/github
cd ~/github
From now on, all mkdir some-new-project commands in this tutorial assume you are inside ~/github/.



1. Create a project folder
mkdir my-research
cd my-research


2. Initialize Git
git init
This creates a hidden .git/ folder inside your project. That folder stores all version history. You never need to touch it directly.


3. Check the status
git status
Git will tell you that you are on branch main and there are no commits yet. This is your starting point.

title: Session 4: Git Fundamentals
text:
Staging and Committing

Create a file






WarningDo not use TextEdit or Notepad for code files



macOS TextEdit defaults to rich text (RTF) and silently adds .txt to your filenames, so clean_data.R becomes clean_data.R.txt with invisible formatting markup inside. Windows Notepad has similar encoding and line-ending issues that break code files.
Use RStudio (File → New File → R Script) or VS Code (File → New File) instead. Both are installed as part of the Preliminaries setup and handle plain-text code files correctly. For Terminal users, running code . inside your project folder opens the whole folder in VS Code in one step.


Open your text editor (RStudio or VS Code) and create a file called clean_data.R with the following content:
# clean_data.R
# Load and clean the wage dataset
# Uses wooldridge::wage1 (Jeffrey Wooldridge's teaching dataset; 526 obs)

wages <- wooldridge::wage1

# Remove observations with missing wages (none in wage1, but real data always needs this)
wages <- wages[!is.na(wages$wage), ]

# Log transformation
wages$log_wage <- log(wages$wage)
Save the file in your my-research folder.


Stage the file






NoteThree interfaces, same Git





From here on, command blocks appear in tabs: Terminal, RStudio, and VS Code. All three do exactly the same thing. In class we walk through the Terminal tab on the projector.
Why Terminal for teaching? Four reasons worth naming up front.

The mental model is cleaner. edit → stage → commit is three explicit commands. In a GUI, staging is often just a checkbox, and students never really register what the operation is until something breaks. A rigorous researcher needs to understand the operation, not just the button.
Terminal Git works everywhere. GUIs do not. You will eventually use Cornell’s CAC cluster, FSRDC secure computing environments, AWS or Google Cloud VMs, or any other remote machine. None of those have a GUI. A researcher who only knows the RStudio Git pane is stuck the first time they work with a dataset that does not fit on their laptop.
Documentation, error messages, and AI tools all speak command-line. Every answer on Stack Overflow, every chapter of the Pro Git book and Happy Git with R, every response from Claude Code, GitHub Copilot, and Cursor will tell you to run a Git command. If the only Git you know is “I clicked the button in RStudio”, you cannot use any of those resources to get unstuck.
Git is scriptable. Researchers use system("git log --pretty=format:%h") inside R to embed a commit hash in an output file for reproducibility. They run git archive inside a Makefile to package data. They call git tag automatically from a submission script at paper-submission time. None of this is available through a GUI.

The bottom line. We teach commands in class so you have the vocabulary, the portability, and the safety net when things go wrong. Once you understand what each command does, feel free to use RStudio or VS Code for daily work. The tabs on this page show the equivalent actions in each interface.




TerminalRStudioVS Code


git add clean_data.R


Click Commit in the Git pane (top-right of the IDE). The Review Changes dialog opens. Select clean_data.R in the top pane. The bottom pane shows your diff. Click the Stage button, or tick the checkbox next to the file. It moves from the Unstaged list to the Staged list.
Quick shortcut. If you already know what you are staging and do not need to see the diff, tick the Staged checkbox directly in the Git pane without opening the dialog.
No Git pane? The project must be an RStudio Project (.Rproj) inside a Git repository. Tools → Project Options → Git/SVN → Version control system: Git, then restart RStudio.


Open the Source Control panel (branch icon in the left activity bar, or ⌃⇧G / Ctrl+Shift+G). Under Changes, click clean_data.R. A diff view opens in the editor showing your changes. In the Source Control panel, hover over clean_data.R and click the + icon to stage. The file moves from Changes to Staged Changes.
Quick shortcut. If you already know what you are staging, click the + directly without previewing the diff.



All three do the same thing: they tell Git “include this file in the next commit.”


Check the status again
git status
You should see clean_data.R listed under “Changes to be committed.”






TipYour editor shows the same state in real time



If you have VS Code or RStudio open on this folder right now, they are already displaying everything git status just told you. You do not need to run the command to know the state of your repo; your editor is watching.
VS Code. The Source Control panel (branch icon, left activity bar) lists all modified and staged files, updating live. The file tree shows letters next to each file: U for untracked, M for modified, A for added/staged. Open a file and the gutter next to the line numbers shows green/blue/red bars for added, modified, and deleted lines compared to the last commit.
RStudio. The Git pane (top-right) shows the same information with status icons. It refreshes automatically every time you save a file or run a Git command in the Terminal.
This is one of the underrated benefits of keeping an editor open on your project: the Git state is ambient, not something you have to query. You can run Terminal commands in class and watch the editor reflect each change instantly.




Commit
git commit -m "Add data cleaning script"
The -m flag lets you write the commit message inline. Every commit needs a message that explains why you made the change.


Make another change and commit
Edit clean_data.R. For example, add a new line:
# Keep only workers with at least a high school education
wages <- wages[wages$educ >= 12, ]
Then stage and commit:
git add clean_data.R
git commit -m "Filter to workers with high school education or more"


Writing good commits
A commit has two parts: what changed (the files you staged) and why (the message). Both matter.
Be specific in the message. “Fix bug” is unhelpful. “Fix off-by-one error in sample selection” is useful. Your future self, three months from now, will thank you. Your co-authors will thank you even more.
One logical change per commit. If you need the word “and” to describe what a commit does, it is probably two commits. Good messages read like a research changelog:
Add control for state fixed effects
Switch to winsorized outcome at 1%
Fix sample filter for pre-2000 observations
Mixing unrelated changes in one commit makes it impossible to revert just one of them later.
Back to the shopping cart. Each commit is an order. If an order contains one coherent purchase (all the groceries for the week), returning it makes sense. If it contains a book, a stapler, and a bag of flour lumped together, returning just one item is painful. Before committing, ask yourself: if I had to undo just this commit six months from now, would that make sense as a unit?
Different styles exist. Researchers differ in how granular their commits are:

Atomic commits. Every small change is a commit. Very detailed history. Easy to review and revert. Standard in open source software. Recommended by Jenny Bryan in Happy Git with R, the canonical Git guide for R users.
Feature commits. One commit per completed task, for example “baseline regression table done”. Bigger commits but still coherent. More common in solo research.
End-of-session commits. One commit per day, bundling everything. Tempting but loses the ability to audit what you did and where.

For PhD research, aim between atomic and feature. Commit after each logical step in your workflow: data pulled, sample selected, variables built, model estimated, figure produced. Roughly one commit per line you would write in a research diary.
When to commit. Whenever the code runs and produces the intended result after your change. Also before starting something risky (a refactor, an alternative specification), and before you stop working for the day.
Avoid these messages. "Fixed stuff", "WIP", "Lots of changes", ".". They mean you skipped thinking about what you changed. Either split the commit into pieces with meaningful messages, or think for another ten seconds about what to write.

title: Session 4: Git Fundamentals
section: Exercise 1: Create a Project and Make Commits
text:
Exercise 1: Create a Project and Make Commits
Time: ~15 minutes

Create a folder called wage-analysis and initialize Git:

mkdir wage-analysis
cd wage-analysis
git init

Create a file called clean_data.R. Write a few lines of R code (anything is fine: loading a library, creating a variable, etc.).
Stage and commit:

git add clean_data.R
git commit -m "Add data cleaning script"

Edit the file (add a comment or a new line of code), then stage and commit again:

git add clean_data.R
git commit -m "Add full-time worker filter"

View your commits:

git log
You should see two commits with your messages, timestamps, and author info.

title: Session 4: Git Fundamentals
text:
Viewing History

git log
git log shows the full history of commits, most recent first. Each entry includes the commit hash (a unique ID), author, date, and message.
For a compact view:
git log --oneline
Output looks like:
a1b2c3d  Add regression output table
e4f5g6h  Clean variable names
i7j8k9l  Add data cleaning script


git diff
git diff shows what changed since the last commit (for unstaged files):
git diff
Example output:
- lm(wage ~ educ, data = df)
+ lm(wage ~ educ + exper, data = df)
Lines starting with - were removed. Lines starting with + were added.
To see changes that are already staged:
git diff --staged

title: Session 4: Git Fundamentals
text:
.gitignore
Not every file belongs in version control. Large data files, sensitive credentials, and system files should be excluded.
Create a file called .gitignore in your project root:
# Data files (too large or sensitive for Git)
*.csv
*.dta
data/

# R artifacts
.Rhistory
.RData
.Rproj.user/

# System files
.DS_Store
Thumbs.db
Then stage and commit it:
git add .gitignore
git commit -m "Add .gitignore for data and R artifacts"






Warning



Data files do not belong in Git. Git is designed for code (small text files), not for large datasets. If you commit a 500 MB CSV, every collaborator will have to download it with the full repository. Use a shared drive, Dropbox, or a data repository for large files.

title: Session 4: Git Fundamentals
text:
Undoing Mistakes
You will make mistakes. Git has tools to recover from each of them.

Unstage a file
You staged a file by accident and want to remove it from the staging area (without deleting the file):
git restore --staged clean_data.R


Discard local changes
You edited a file and want to go back to the last committed version:
git restore clean_data.R






Caution



This is destructive. Your edits are gone. Git cannot recover them because they were never committed.




Undo the last commit (keep changes)
You committed too early and want to undo the commit but keep the files staged:
git reset --soft HEAD~1
HEAD~1 means “one commit before the current one.”

title: Session 4: Git Fundamentals
text:
Exercise 2: Full Workflow Practice
Time: ~15 minutes

In your wage-analysis project, create a .gitignore file with the contents shown above. Stage and commit it.
Create a new file called run_regression.R:

# run_regression.R
# Estimate a Mincer wage equation on wooldridge::wage1

wages <- wooldridge::wage1

# Basic OLS
model1 <- lm(log(wage) ~ educ + exper + tenure, data = wages)
summary(model1)
Stage and commit:
git add run_regression.R
git commit -m "Add basic wage regression"

Edit run_regression.R and add a second model:

# Add experience squared
model2 <- lm(log(wage) ~ educ + exper + I(exper^2) + tenure, data = wages)
summary(model2)
View the diff before committing:
git diff
Then stage and commit:
git add run_regression.R
git commit -m "Add quadratic experience specification"

Practice unstaging. Create a junk file, stage it, then unstage:

echo "test" > junk.R
git add junk.R
git status
git restore --staged junk.R
git status

View your full history:

git log --oneline

title: Session 4: Git Fundamentals
text:
What’s Next
In Session 5 we will connect your local repository to GitHub, learn to push and pull, create branches, and collaborate with pull requests.
Before next session: create a free account at github.com.

title: Session 5: GitHub & Collaboration
text:
In Session 4 we learned the core local Git workflow:

git init: create a repository
git add / git commit: stage and save snapshots
git log / git diff: view history and changes
.gitignore: exclude files from tracking
git restore / git reset: undo mistakes

Today we take those skills online.

title: Session 5: GitHub & Collaboration
text:
In Session 4 we learned the core local Git workflow:

git init: create a repository
git add / git commit: stage and save snapshots
git log / git diff: view history and changes
.gitignore: exclude files from tracking
git restore / git reset: undo mistakes

Today we take those skills online.

title: Session 5: GitHub & Collaboration
text:
What Is GitHub?
GitHub is a cloud hosting service for Git repositories. It provides:

Backup: your code lives in the cloud, not just on your laptop
Collaboration: co-authors can contribute to the same project
Sharing: publish replication packages and make research transparent







Note



GitHub is free for public and private repositories. You can also apply for GitHub Education benefits as a student or faculty member.

title: Session 5: GitHub & Collaboration
text:
Keep Your Email Private
Every commit you push to a public GitHub repository shows the name and email you configured back in Session 4. If you used a personal email, it is now visible to anyone browsing the repo. GitHub offers a stable noreply address tied to your account that you can use instead.

Go to github.com/settings/emails.
Under Primary email address, check the box for Keep my email addresses private.
GitHub shows a noreply address shaped like 12345678+yourusername@users.noreply.github.com. Copy it.
Update your local Git configuration:

git config --global user.email "12345678+yourusername@users.noreply.github.com"
Replace the address with the one shown on your GitHub settings page.






Tip



This only affects commits made after you change the setting. Past commits keep whatever email they were created with. Rewriting history to replace old emails is possible but advanced. See GitHub’s documentation if you need it.

title: Session 5: GitHub & Collaboration
text:
Connect to GitHub: SSH Keys
SSH keys let your computer authenticate with GitHub without typing a password every time.

1. Generate a key pair
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "you@email.com"
Press Enter to accept the default file location. You can set a passphrase or leave it empty.


2. Start the SSH agent and add your key
eval "$(ssh-agent -s)"
ssh-add ~/.ssh/id_ed25519


3. Copy the public key
cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub
Copy the entire output.


4. Add the key to GitHub

Go to github.com → click your profile picture → Settings
In the left sidebar, click SSH and GPG keys
Click New SSH key
Paste your key and give it a title (e.g., “My Laptop”)
Click Add SSH key



5. Test the connection
ssh -T git@github.com
You should see: Hi username! You've successfully authenticated...






Tip



Windows users: run these commands in Git Bash, not in PowerShell or Command Prompt.

title: Session 5: GitHub & Collaboration
text:
Remotes: Push and Pull
A remote is a copy of your repository stored on a server (like GitHub). The standard remote name is origin.

The relationship
Your Computer  ←— git pull —→  GitHub (origin)
               —— git push ——→

git push uploads your commits to GitHub
git pull downloads new commits from GitHub



Link your local repo to GitHub
First, create a new repository on GitHub:

Go to github.com/new
Name it (e.g., wage-analysis)
Do NOT initialize with a README (your local repo already has commits)
Click Create repository

GitHub will show you setup commands. Use the SSH option:
git remote add origin git@github.com:yourusername/wage-analysis.git


Push your commits
git push -u origin main
The -u flag sets origin main as the default upstream, so future pushes only need git push.


Pull changes
If someone else pushed commits (or you edited a file on GitHub), download them:
git pull origin main

title: Session 5: GitHub & Collaboration
text:
Cloning a Repository
git clone downloads an entire repository (with full history) from GitHub to your computer:
git clone git@github.com:username/wage-analysis.git
cd wage-analysis
After cloning, the remote origin is already configured. You can immediately push and pull.






Tip



Cloning is how you start working on a collaborator’s project. You get the full history, all branches, and a ready-to-go remote connection.

title: Session 5: GitHub & Collaboration
text:
Exercise 3: Push to GitHub
Time: ~15 minutes

Create a repository on GitHub. Go to github.com/new, name it wage-analysis, and click Create (do NOT initialize with README).
Add the remote to your local wage-analysis project:

cd wage-analysis
git remote add origin git@github.com:YOURUSERNAME/wage-analysis.git
Replace YOURUSERNAME with your GitHub username.

Push your commits:

git push -u origin main

Verify. Go to your repository page on GitHub and refresh. You should see your files and commit history.
Test pulling. On GitHub, click on clean_data.R → pencil icon → add a comment line at the top → Commit. Then pull locally:

git pull origin main
Open the file to confirm the edit is there.

title: Session 5: GitHub & Collaboration
text:
Branches
A branch is a parallel version of your code. You can experiment on a branch without affecting the main version. When the work is ready, you merge it back.

Why branches?

Try a new specification without breaking working code
Each co-author works on their own branch
Keep main clean and functional at all times



Create and switch to a branch
git branch add-iv-analysis
git checkout add-iv-analysis
Or, as a shortcut:
git checkout -b add-iv-analysis


Work on the branch
Make your edits, stage, and commit as usual. These commits only exist on the branch.
# Create a new file
echo '# IV regression using 2SLS' > iv_analysis.R
git add iv_analysis.R
git commit -m "Add IV analysis script"


Switch back to main
git checkout main
Notice that iv_analysis.R disappears. It only exists on the other branch. Your working directory reflects whichever branch you are on.


Merge the branch
When you are satisfied with the work on your branch:
git checkout main
git merge add-iv-analysis
Now iv_analysis.R appears on main and all the branch’s commits are part of main’s history.


Delete the branch (optional)
After merging, you can clean up:
git branch -d add-iv-analysis

title: Session 5: GitHub & Collaboration
text:
Pull Requests
A pull request (PR) is GitHub’s way of proposing changes. Instead of merging locally, you push a branch to GitHub and ask for it to be reviewed before merging.

The pull request workflow

Create a branch and make your commits
Push the branch to GitHub:

git push -u origin add-iv-analysis

Open a PR on GitHub. Go to your repository → you will see a banner saying “add-iv-analysis had recent pushes” → click Compare & pull request
Write a description explaining what you changed and why
Review. Your co-author reads the changes, leaves comments, and approves
Merge. Click Merge pull request on GitHub







Note



Pull requests are the standard collaboration workflow in both industry and academic research. They create a written record of what changed and why, which is valuable for reproducibility.

title: Session 5: GitHub & Collaboration
text:
Handling Merge Conflicts
A merge conflict happens when two people edit the same line in the same file. Git marks the conflict and asks you to choose.

What a conflict looks like
<<<<<<< HEAD
lm(wage ~ educ + exper, data = df)
=======
lm(log(wage) ~ educ + exper + tenure, data = df)
>>>>>>> add-iv-analysis

The top section (HEAD) is your current branch’s version
The bottom section is the incoming branch’s version



How to resolve

Open the file in your editor
Decide which version to keep (or combine them into something new)
Delete the <<<<<<<, =======, and >>>>>>> markers
Stage and commit the resolved file:

git add clean_data.R
git commit -m "Resolve merge conflict in regression specification"






Tip



Merge conflicts are normal, not dangerous. They happen whenever two people edit the same line. The fix is always: open the file, choose the right version, remove the markers, commit.

title: Session 5: GitHub & Collaboration
text:
Exercise 4: Branches and Pull Requests
Time: ~20 minutes. Work with a partner.

Setup

Person A: go to your wage-analysis repo on GitHub → Settings → Collaborators → Add your partner’s GitHub username.
Person B: accept the invitation (check your email or GitHub notifications), then clone Person A’s repo:

git clone git@github.com:PERSONA_USERNAME/wage-analysis.git
cd wage-analysis


Create branches and PRs

Both partners: create a branch with your name, add a new .R file, commit, and push:

git checkout -b yourname-feature
Create a file (e.g., descriptive_stats.R or robustness_check.R) with a few lines of R code.
git add your_file.R
git commit -m "Add descriptive statistics"
git push -u origin yourname-feature

Both partners: go to GitHub and open a pull request from your branch into main.
Review each other’s PR. Click on “Files changed” to see the diff. Leave a comment (e.g., “Looks good!” or “Maybe add a header comment?”). Then click Merge pull request.
Pull the merged changes locally:

git checkout main
git pull origin main
Both files should now be on main.

title: Session 5: GitHub & Collaboration
text:
Tags for Reproducibility
Tags mark a specific point in history. They are the mechanism for recording the code that produced a given version of your paper: the first submission, the R&R revision, the final accepted manuscript, the posted replication package. Years later you can return to any of those versions in one command.
A typical research project has a tag for each submission milestone:
v0.1-first-submission       (code used in the initial JPE submission)
v0.2-first-RR               (code for the first round of R&R)
v0.3-second-RR              (code for the second round)
v1.0-accepted               (final version in the published paper)
v1.0-replication-package    (the archive you posted on AEA/Zenodo/ICPSR)

Create a tag
git tag -a v0.1-first-submission -m "Code for initial JPE submission"


Push tags to GitHub
git push --tags


Check out a tag later
git checkout v1.0
This puts you in “detached HEAD” state. You can look around and run code, but any new commits won’t belong to a branch. To go back:
git checkout main


GitHub Releases
On GitHub, tags appear under the Releases tab. You can attach files (data documentation, README) to a release, making it a self-contained replication package.






Important



Many journals now require replication packages. A tagged GitHub release with a README is a clean, standard way to comply.

title: Session 5: GitHub & Collaboration
text:
Summary
You now have the tools to:



Task
Commands




Track changes locally
git init, add, commit


View history
git log, diff


Back up to GitHub
git remote add, push, pull


Collaborate
git branch, checkout, merge


Review changes
Pull requests on GitHub


Mark reproducible versions
git tag




Next steps

Put your current research project on GitHub today
Use branches for every new analysis or robustness check
Write meaningful commit messages
Bookmark this companion website for reference



Resources

Pro Git book (free online)
GitHub Docs
Happy Git with R by Jenny Bryan: an excellent guide for R users

title: Git & GitHub Cheat Sheet
text:
Command
What it does




git config --global user.name "Name"
Set your name for commits


git config --global user.email "email"
Set your email for commits


git init
Create a new repository in the current folder


git clone <url>
Download a remote repository to your computer

title: Git & GitHub Cheat Sheet
text:
Command
What it does




git config --global user.name "Name"
Set your name for commits


git config --global user.email "email"
Set your email for commits


git init
Create a new repository in the current folder


git clone <url>
Download a remote repository to your computer

title: Git & GitHub Cheat Sheet
text:
Daily Workflow







Command
What it does




git status
Show which files are modified, staged, or untracked


git add <file>
Stage a file for the next commit


git add .
Stage all changed files


git commit -m "message"
Save a snapshot with a descriptive message


git diff
Show unstaged changes


git diff --staged
Show staged changes (what will be committed)


git log --oneline
View commit history (compact)


git log
View commit history (detailed)

title: Git & GitHub Cheat Sheet
text:
Undoing Things







Command
What it does




git restore --staged <file>
Unstage a file (keep edits)


git restore <file>
Discard edits, revert to last commit (destructive)


git reset --soft HEAD~1
Undo last commit (keep files staged)

title: Git & GitHub Cheat Sheet
text:
Remotes (GitHub)



Command
What it does




git remote add origin <url>
Link local repo to GitHub


git push -u origin main
Push commits to GitHub (first time)


git push
Push commits (after -u is set)


git pull origin main
Download new commits from GitHub

title: Git & GitHub Cheat Sheet
Git & GitHub Cheat Sheet
text:
Tags (Reproducibility)



Command
What it does




git tag -a v1.0 -m "msg"
Create an annotated tag


git push --tags
Push all tags to GitHub


git checkout v1.0
Check out a specific tagged version

title: Git & GitHub Cheat Sheet
text:
SSH Setup (One-Time)
# Generate key
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "you@email.com"

# Start agent and add key
eval "$(ssh-agent -s)"
ssh-add ~/.ssh/id_ed25519

# Copy public key (paste into GitHub Settings > SSH Keys)
cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub

# Test connection
ssh -T git@github.com

title: Git & GitHub Cheat Sheet
text:
.gitignore Template for R Projects
# Data files
*.csv
*.dta
*.rds
*.xlsx
data/

# R artifacts
.Rhistory
.RData
.Rproj.user/

# Output (can be regenerated)
*.pdf
*.html
output/

# System files
.DS_Store
Thumbs.db

title: Session 6: AI Foundations
text:
NoteThis session is under development



Content for this session will be added before April 27, 2026. The outline below reflects the planned structure. Version control is covered in Sessions 4–5.

title: Session 6: AI Foundations
text:
Planned content

The AI landscape for researchers (~15 min)

Chat interfaces: Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini. Who makes what.
Coding assistants: Claude Code (Anthropic), GitHub Copilot (GitHub / Microsoft; uses multiple backend models), Cursor (Anysphere; an IDE that wraps Claude, GPT, Gemini), Codex (OpenAI).
Models vs. products. Some of these are models; others are products that route to models. The practical implication is that when you document AI use for replication, you record both.
How LLMs work at a high level. Tokens, context windows, model families, the gap between training data and current reality.



AI for coding: live demo (~25 min)

Write R code for a realistic data task with an LLM.
The workflow: prompt, review, iterate, verify.
Common failure modes: hallucinated functions, wrong package syntax, subtle statistical errors.
How version control (Session 4–5) is the safety net: every AI change is a commit you can review and revert.



Best practices for AI-assisted research code (~15 min)

Verify every output before committing.
Use AI as a first draft, not a final answer.
Prompt engineering basics: be specific, provide context, ask for explanations.
Treat AI suggestions with the same skepticism you would apply to a well-meaning but unfamiliar RA.



Hands-on exercise (~15 min)

Students use an LLM to write an R script for a simple task.
Compare outputs across tools.
Commit the result to their Git repo.



Wrap-up (~5 min)

Preview of Session 7: journal disclosure policies and ethical scenarios.
